title: Know That You Know
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Know That You Know - Pastor Tito Lyro - 1 John 2_3-11.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Desire for Certainty
subpoints: Everyone wants certainty — uncertainty is what drives anxiety
John promises at least one thing we can be certain of: our salvation
He provides tests for genuine faith — the obedience test and the love test
John wrote 1 John to help believers know that they know Jesus

outline.title: To Know Him: The Biblical Meaning
subpoints: Biblical knowledge is intimate and personal, not merely cognitive
To know someone in Scripture is to be in a deep, personal relationship with them
Knowing, being in Him, abiding in Him, and being in the light are all equivalent
John wrote his Gospel that we would come to know Jesus; 1 John assures us we have known Him

outline.title: The Obedience Test: Do You Keep His Commandments?
subpoints: The proof that you truly know God is in the pudding of your obedience
The test of faith is not feelings but behavioral fruit
Those who claim to know Him but do not keep His commandments are liars
John is not talking about the struggle with sin — he is talking about the absence of the struggle

outline.title: The Love Test: Abiding in the Light
subpoints: The one who hates his brother is in darkness and walks in darkness
Love for God and love for the brethren are not identical but are inseparably connected
The vine gives life to the branches — abiding in Christ produces the fruit of love
To abide in Christ is to be rooted in Him through the ordinary means of grace

outline.title: Pursuing Assurance: Which Group Are You In?
subpoints: Assurance is attainable, though not of the essence of saving faith
There are four groups: those who believe and know it, those who believe but struggle, those who don't believe and know it, and those who don't believe but think they do
Three questions to seek assurance: Do you obey God's Word with a joyful heart? Do you love the brethren? Do you believe the Jesus of the Bible?
Repentance is the simple — though not easy — path forward for those who doubt

summary:
In this sermon on 1 John 2:3-11, Pastor Tito Lyro takes up John's concern to give believers assurance of salvation — to help them 'know that they know' Jesus. The sermon begins by noting that while everyone desires certainty, genuine assurance of saving faith is available. John provides two tests in this passage: the obedience test and the love test.

The obedience test is grounded in John's distinctly biblical use of the word 'know' — knowing God is not merely cognitive familiarity but intimate personal relationship, as with Adam and Eve. If that relationship is genuine, it will invariably produce obedience to God's Word. Someone who professes faith but whose life is characterized by disregard for Scripture is called a liar by John — not a moralistic term for terrible people, but a sober description of those in the church who claim to know Jesus while persistently disobeying Him. Pastor Lyro is careful to distinguish this from the ongoing struggle with sin that every believer experiences: John is addressing the absence of the struggle, not the presence of it.

The love test follows: the one who says he is in the light but hates his brother is still in darkness. Both tests point to abiding in Christ — remaining rooted in Him through the ordinary means of grace (preaching, the Lord's Table, baptism, prayer, and fellowship), which is what produces the fruit of obedience and love. The sermon closes with four groups identified in every local church — including the most dangerous: those who sincerely think they are believers but are not. Three diagnostic questions help hearers evaluate themselves, and repentance is held out as the simple (not easy) path for those who recognize their profession has not matched their practice.
transcript:
## Introduction

I think it is fair to say that we all like certainty. We want to know what is next. We prefer not being surprised by life. That is why we get anxious — because there is a lack of certainty. Another word for certainty is assurance. We want to be sure of things.

John says in this passage that there is at least one thing we can be sure of, and that is our salvation. John gives us in this passage a couple of tests for us to examine our claim to faith. He is helping us know that we know. He is trying to help us have the tools to know that our faith is real, to know that we know Jesus.

Later on, John will add a third test to the two we see in this passage. In this passage he talks about the test of obedience — is what you believe generating obedience in your life? — and the test of love — is your faith in Christ causing you to love the brethren? We are going to look at the first one this morning.

John employs the same affirmations-and-denials structure that he used in chapter 1 — saying, I mean this and not that. If your faith is real, this is what it is going to look like. If your faith is real, this is how it is not going to look like. I find that very helpful.

## What Does It Mean to "Know" Jesus?

John starts in chapter 2:3: "Now by this we know that we know Him." To know that you know is to be assured of the thing that you know. And the object of that knowledge is "Him" — which verse 6 clarifies is Jesus Christ, the one who walked among us.

But the idea of knowing in the Bible goes beyond having cognitive knowledge about a person. In Genesis 4:1, the Bible tells us that Adam knew Eve — and the result of that knowledge was that she got pregnant. In Amos 3:2, God says he "knew" Israel of all the families of the earth — this is language of a special, intimate relationship, not mere factual knowledge.

To know someone in the context of 1 John is to have a deep, personal, intimate relationship with that person — to be united to that person. And in this passage, knowing him is equivalent to being in him (verse 5), abiding in him (verse 6), and being in the light (verse 9). All different ways of describing the same reality: believing in the Lord Jesus Christ.

John wrote his Gospel so that you would believe in Jesus:

> *"But these are written that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that believing you may have life in His name."* (John 20:31)

And he writes 1 John as a companion letter so that those who have believed may know that they have believed:

> *"These things I have written to you who believe in the name of the Son of God, that you may know that you have eternal life."* (1 John 5:13)

The false teachers who had come into the church were claiming special knowledge — some secret information that nobody else had. John turns that around and uses the claim of knowledge to show the church they do not need any special knowledge. They already know how to have a relationship with Jesus Christ. And he wants them to know that they know that.

## The Test of Obedience

How do you know that you know Jesus? Verse 3: "Now by this we know that we know Him, if we keep His commandments." John says the proof that you truly know God is in the pudding of your obedience. The proof of faith is in the doing of it, in your obeying the Lord Jesus Christ.

I want you to notice that John does not say anything about how you feel about Jesus. Warm, fuzzy feelings — that is not the test. The authentic claims of knowing God in verse 3, living in Christ in verse 6, and being in the light in verse 9 will invariably show themselves in a life of obeying God (verse 4), a life of imitating Christ (verse 6), and a life of loving the brethren (verses 9–10).

Jesus himself says the same thing in John 14:15: "If you love Me, keep My commandments." Love for Jesus is not equal to obedience — but love for Jesus will always result in obedience. They can never be divorced from one another. This is not the only test that John gives, but it is an important one alongside the love test and the belief test.

## The Denial: The Liar

John follows up the affirmation with the denial. Verse 4:

> *"He who says, 'I know Him,' and does not keep His commandments, is a liar, and the truth is not in him."* (1 John 2:4)

The term the Bible uses for someone who says "I know Jesus, I love him, he is my Savior" while having no regard for what the Bible says — is liar. Patrick Mino once said in a counseling context: "Your life is speaking so loudly that I cannot hear your words." Our lives have to speak the same thing as our words when we claim faith in the Lord Jesus Christ.

Now, the person in verse 4 does not have to be ranked up there with the names we usually list when we want to evoke images of the truly wicked. This person is probably in the church. John is not talking about someone out there. And the sins we are talking about are not only sins of commission (doing what God forbids — adultery, fornication, and so on), but also sins of omission — not doing what God commands. Not loving your wife. Not submitting to your husband. Refusing to be reconciled. Being bitter. Being characterized by a lack of love and kindness to the brethren.

Important: John is not talking about the struggle with sin that every believer goes through in daily life. There is not a day that ends without a struggle with sin. John is talking about the lack of struggle — the person who claims to believe in Jesus and just goes on sinning without caring, without repenting. Sinning without repentance is what John is addressing here.

## Keeping His Word

And so that we do not think he is speaking of a specific set of commandments, John clarifies in verse 5: "Whoever keeps His word, truly the love of God is perfected in him." He uses the word "word" rather than "commandments" because he knows we are all lawyers at heart — we will try to redefine things to fit our context. So he says: I mean from Genesis 1 to Revelation 22. All of it.

He also says that by keeping his word, "the love of God is perfected in him." This love is not the love God has for us — that does not need to be perfected. It is the love we have for God. Our love for God is made complete, is perfected, by our obedience to his Word. The inward reality of our hearts is displayed in our outward actions.

As 1 John 5:3 says: "This is the love of God, that we keep His commandments. And His commandments are not burdensome." Not burdensome — because they come from a God who saved us in Jesus Christ, who only gives us what is good. "Come to Me, all you who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. My yoke is easy and My burden is light" (Matthew 11:28–30).

But our love for him is always a response to his love for us. 1 John 4:9–10:

> *"In this the love of God was manifested toward us, that God has sent His only begotten Son into the world, that we might live through Him. In this is love, not that we loved God, but that He loved us and sent His Son to be the propitiation for our sins."* (1 John 4:9–10)

We love God because God loved us. You respond to love demonstrated on the cross of Calvary — the death that was yours, and yet Christ took upon himself. You respond to that. Then we obey and we love him. F.F. Bruce: obedience is the full flowering of our love for Jesus.

## Abiding in Christ

Verse 6: "He who says he abides in Him ought himself also to walk just as He walked." To abide means to remain or dwell — here, in a person. If you abide in him, your behavior will resemble his. How does that work? The life of Christ in his people will be manifested as their behavior resembles him. How do we abide? By being rooted into him and by feeding on him through the ordinary means of grace — the preaching of the Word, the Lord's Table, baptism, prayer, fellowship of the saints. Not the extraordinary. Not being zapped from heaven. But the everyday walk with Christ, with his people, being part of his church.

John 15:4–5:

> *"Abide in Me, and I in you. As the branch cannot bear fruit of itself, unless it abides in the vine, neither can you, unless you abide in Me. I am the vine, you are the branches. He who abides in Me, and I in him, bears much fruit; for without Me you can do nothing."* (John 15:4–5)

If someone is in Christ, he cannot go any other way but the way of obedience — because Christ is compelling him to go that way. It is not that you have to do it on your own. Christ is doing it for you, in you. That is what the vine does for the branches. And that is why if you claim to abide in Christ and yet there is no evidence in your life of that, you have no reason to be confident in that claim.

## How Did Jesus Walk?

If we are to walk as he walked, we need to know how he walked. Just two things: he walked full of grace and full of truth. John 1:14: "And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld His glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth." These will be the characteristics of those who abide in him as well.

## Conclusion: Know That You Know

Assurance of faith is attainable, though it is not of the essence of faith. You can be a true believer who is not sure of your salvation — but you are commanded to seek that assurance. John wants the true believer to know that he or she belongs to Christ.

There are four groups of people in the world, as far as faith in Christ goes. Those who believe in Jesus and know that their faith is real, and are therefore assured. Those who believe in Jesus but struggle to know the reality of their faith. Those who do not believe in Jesus and do not care that they do not. And the fourth group — the most dangerous: those who do not believe in Jesus but either pretend to, or are misguided in their thinking. If you are in that group, you are headed to judgment and you do not know it.

Claims of faith result in actions of faith. Obedience is the result of our faith in Jesus Christ. Are you struggling to know if your faith is real? Ask yourself three questions:

Are you obeying God's Word with a joyful heart? Is the movie of your life, since you came to faith in Jesus Christ, a display of growth — however small, however slow — in being like Christ? Sometimes it is 0.1 on the Richter scale. Sometimes it is just facing the right direction. That is all growth.

Do you love the brethren? Not people in general while hating these people specifically — but can you generally say, I love God's people?

Do you believe the Jesus of the Bible? That he is both God and man in one person, who lived the perfect life you cannot live, who died the death separated from his Father that you deserve for your sins, and who gloriously came back to life to give you the fullness of life?

Do you know that Jesus? Then you can know that you know him. You can be assured that God is working a great work in your heart, and he will complete that work at the return of the Lord Jesus Christ — when your perfected soul and your perfected body will be united together in fellowship with Christ forever.
